clauses.title: AUTOSAR Cybersecurity Overview
clauses.guidance:
AUTOSAR (AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture) provides the standardized software architecture for automotive ECUs, including a comprehensive set of security modules that implement the technical controls required by ISO 21434 and UN R155. The security architecture spans communication protection, cryptographic services, key management, intrusion detection, and verified boot — together forming the ECU-level implementation of a vehicle's cybersecurity concept.

Security Module Landscape. The core security modules are: SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication) for message authentication on CAN, CAN FD, FlexRay, and Ethernet; the Crypto Stack (CSM, CryIf, Crypto Drivers) for standardized cryptographic operations with hardware acceleration support; KeyM (Key Manager) for key lifecycle and certificate handling; IdsM (Intrusion Detection System Manager) for security event aggregation and reporting; and the Secure Boot chain for software integrity verification. These modules interact through well-defined AUTOSAR interfaces — SecOC calls CSM for MAC operations, CSM routes through CryIf to hardware or software crypto drivers, KeyM provisions key material into CSM key elements, and IdsM collects security events from all other modules.

Integration with E/E Architecture. Modern vehicle E/E architectures are transitioning from domain-based to zonal topologies with central compute platforms. AUTOSAR Adaptive (AP) runs on high-performance central processors handling SOME/IP service communication, while AUTOSAR Classic Platform (CP) runs on resource-constrained microcontrollers handling real-time CAN/LIN communication. Security modules exist in both platforms but with different implementation characteristics. CP security modules operate under strict real-time constraints — SecOC MAC computation must complete within the CAN message cycle time. AP security uses ara::crypto and ara::iam service interfaces with more computational headroom.

Configuration Methodology. AUTOSAR security configuration is tool-driven through ARXML (AUTOSAR XML) configuration files processed by the ECU configuration tool (e.g., DaVinci Configurator, EB tresos, ISOLAR). Security-relevant configuration parameters — MAC truncation lengths, key references, freshness value strategies, boot chain verification keys, IDS event taxonomies — are defined in ARXML and compiled into the BSW configuration. A common risk is misconfiguration: incorrect key references, mismatched truncation lengths between sender and receiver, or development-mode overrides left active in production builds. Security configuration review should be a mandatory quality gate before SOP (Start of Production).

R155 and ISO 21434 Mapping. Each AUTOSAR security module maps to specific R155 threats and ISO 21434 work products. SecOC addresses communication integrity (R155 7.3.4), the Crypto Stack supports cryptographic controls (R155 7.3.8), IdsM enables threat monitoring (R155 7.2.2.2(g)), and Secure Boot prevents unauthorized code execution (R155 7.3.4). The AUTOSAR security configuration collectively forms a significant portion of the technical evidence required for R155 type approval — demonstrating that the vehicle type implements specific countermeasures against the identified threats from the TARA.

clauses.title: Crypto Service Manager
clauses.guidance:
The Crypto Service Manager (CSM) is the AUTOSAR module that provides a standardized API for cryptographic operations to all application and basic software modules. It serves as the abstraction layer between consumers of cryptographic services (SecOC, TLS, SecureBoot, KeyM, application SWCs) and the underlying crypto drivers that perform the actual computations.

API Model and Job Processing. CSM uses a job-based API model where each cryptographic operation is defined as a job with a specific configuration. Application modules call CSM service functions such as Csm_MacGenerate, Csm_MacVerify, Csm_Encrypt, Csm_Decrypt, Csm_Hash, Csm_SignatureGenerate, Csm_SignatureVerify, and Csm_RandomGenerate. Each call references a job ID that maps to a preconfigured job definition specifying the algorithm, key element, and processing mode. Jobs support both synchronous and asynchronous processing modes. Synchronous processing blocks the caller until the result is available — suitable for short operations on fast crypto hardware. Asynchronous processing returns immediately and delivers results via a callback function (Csm_CallbackNotification), which is appropriate for longer operations or when the crypto driver delegates to a hardware accelerator with its own processing queue.

Key Element Management. CSM manages cryptographic key material through key elements. A key element is a structured container holding the key bytes, key length, and usage attributes. The Csm_KeyElementSet() function provisions key material into a key element, while Csm_KeyElementGet() retrieves it (subject to access restrictions). Key elements are organized into key groups, and each CSM job references a specific key element. The key element abstraction allows the same API to work whether the underlying key is stored in RAM, NVM, or an HSM key slot. Access control attributes on key elements restrict which modules can read, write, or use specific keys — for production systems, application-layer key extraction should be prohibited.

Hardware Crypto Acceleration Routing. CSM routes crypto requests through the Crypto Interface (CryIf) to one or more crypto drivers. The routing is determined by the CryIf channel configuration, which maps CSM jobs to specific crypto driver instances. A typical ECU configuration includes a software crypto driver (Crypto_30 or similar) for general-purpose operations and an HSM crypto driver for operations requiring hardware protection. The routing decision is static (configured at build time), not dynamic. Performance-sensitive operations like SecOC MAC generation on high-frequency CAN messages should be routed to hardware crypto to meet timing constraints. CSM supports priority-based queue management when multiple jobs compete for the same crypto driver instance.

Supported Algorithms and Primitives. CSM supports AES (128, 192, 256-bit) in ECB, CBC, CTR, and GCM modes for symmetric encryption. For MAC generation, CMAC-AES-128 is the standard choice for SecOC, while HMAC-SHA-256 is used for higher-security applications. Asymmetric operations include RSA (2048, 3072, 4096-bit) and ECC (NIST P-256, P-384, Brainpool curves) for signature generation and verification, as well as ECDH for key agreement. Hash functions include SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512. Random number generation interfaces connect to hardware TRNG (True Random Number Generator) sources or DRBG (Deterministic Random Bit Generator) implementations seeded from hardware entropy.

Configuration and Integration. CSM configuration requires defining job tables, key element structures, CryIf channel mappings, and callback functions. Each job entry specifies the algorithm family, algorithm mode, key reference, and processing type. A common integration issue is misconfigured CryIf routing — directing an HSM-only algorithm to a software driver or vice versa. Testing should verify that all configured jobs produce correct results with test vectors and that timing constraints are met under load. CSM is a mandatory dependency for SecOC, KeyM, TLS stack, and secure boot implementations in AUTOSAR. For R155 type approval, the CSM configuration — including algorithm selections, key protection levels, and hardware vs. software routing rationale — should be documented as part of the cybersecurity specifications. The job table serves as a traceability artifact mapping TARA risk treatments to specific cryptographic controls. Integration testing should verify correct operation under concurrent job loads to ensure that real-time constraints are met when multiple BSW modules request cryptographic services simultaneously.

clauses.title: Crypto Stack integration
clauses.guidance:
The AUTOSAR Crypto Stack is a layered architecture providing cryptographic services to all BSW and application modules. It consists of three layers: the Crypto Service Manager (CSM) at the top, the Crypto Interface (CryIf) in the middle, and one or more Crypto Drivers at the bottom. This layered design allows the same application code to work with different crypto implementations — software libraries, hardware security modules, or a combination — without modification.

Layer Architecture. The CSM receives requests from application and BSW modules through standardized service APIs. It manages job queues, enforces access control on key elements, and forwards requests to CryIf. The CryIf layer provides channel-based routing, mapping each CSM job to a specific crypto driver instance. A single ECU may have multiple crypto drivers: a software crypto driver (SWCrypto, often designated Crypto_30) implementing algorithms in C code, and one or more HSM drivers interfacing with hardware crypto accelerators. The CryIf channel configuration determines which driver handles each job, enabling a split where computationally intensive operations (RSA signature verification, AES-GCM bulk encryption) run on dedicated hardware while lighter operations (CRC, simple hashing) use the software path.

Crypto Provider Abstraction Layer (CPAL). The CPAL concept allows crypto driver implementations to be swapped without affecting upper layers. A crypto driver exposes a standardized interface regardless of whether it wraps a software library (OpenSSL-derived, wolfCrypt), an SHE-compliant security extension, or a full HSM with its own key storage and secure environment. From the CSM perspective, all drivers look identical — the difference is in performance characteristics, security guarantees, and key protection levels.

Software vs. HSM Crypto Selection. The decision to route a cryptographic operation to software or hardware depends on several factors. Security sensitivity: operations involving long-term keys (vehicle identity keys, code signing verification keys) should always use HSM-protected key slots where key material never leaves the secure boundary. Performance: SecOC MAC computation for high-frequency CAN messages (e.g., 10ms cycle time) may require hardware acceleration to meet real-time deadlines. Cost: HSM key slot count is limited and HSMs add BOM cost, so not every key can be hardware-protected. A typical production configuration uses HSM for master keys, CMAC key storage, and signature verification, while using the software driver for session key derivation, hash computation, and development/testing scenarios.

Key Storage Mechanisms. Key material flows through the Crypto Stack differently depending on the storage backend. For software crypto drivers, keys are stored in NVM (Non-Volatile Memory) blocks managed by the NvM module, protected by CRC and optionally encrypted at rest. For HSM drivers, keys reside in dedicated HSM key slots that provide hardware-enforced access control — the key bytes never appear in application processor memory. The KeyM module orchestrates key provisioning into the appropriate storage backend. Mixed configurations are common: an HSM stores the master key and derives working keys into protected RAM for the software crypto driver to use during a session.

ISO 21434 and R155 Relevance. The Crypto Stack architecture is a primary implementation control for ISO 21434 Clause 10 (cybersecurity specifications at component level) and R155 Annex 5 threat 7.3.8 (cryptographic controls). Type approval evidence should document the algorithm selections, key protection levels, and the rationale for software vs. hardware routing decisions. The Crypto Stack configuration directly supports the TARA risk treatment argument: specific threats are mitigated by specific cryptographic controls with documented strength levels. Integration testing of the Crypto Stack must verify correct operation across all layers. Common issues include CryIf routing mismatches (directing a job to a driver that does not support the required algorithm), key element reference errors (CSM job referencing a non-existent key element ID), and performance bottlenecks when multiple consumers compete for a single HSM instance. Test vectors from NIST (for AES, SHA) and Brainpool (for ECC curves) should validate cryptographic correctness, while load testing under realistic SecOC traffic patterns verifies timing compliance. The Crypto Stack configuration is auditable — type approval authorities may request the algorithm inventory, key protection level mapping, and HSM utilization analysis as part of the R155 technical assessment.

mappings.notes: Cryptographic controls support CSMS technical measures.
Implementation-level cybersecurity controls.

clauses.title: Secure Diagnostics
clauses.guidance:
Secure diagnostics in AUTOSAR addresses the protection of UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services) communication, which represents one of the most significant attack surfaces on modern vehicles. Diagnostic interfaces provide direct access to ECU functions including firmware programming, memory read/write, calibration modification, and actuator control — capabilities that must be tightly controlled to prevent unauthorized access.

Authentication Mechanisms. AUTOSAR supports two UDS authentication approaches. Legacy SecurityAccess (service 0x27) uses a challenge-response (seed-key) protocol where the ECU sends a random seed and the tester must return a correctly computed key. The algorithm strength depends on the OEM's implementation — older systems used weak proprietary algorithms vulnerable to brute-force and cryptanalytic attacks. Modern implementations should use AES-based key derivation with 128-bit minimum security level and enforce attempt counters with escalating lockout delays (e.g., 10-second delay after 3 failures, power-cycle required after 10 failures). The newer Authentication service (0x29) supports certificate-based authentication with full PKI integration, providing stronger identity verification and enabling fine-grained access control based on certificate attributes.

Authorization and Access Control. AUTOSAR diagnostic security follows a tiered access model where different authentication levels unlock different service sets. Level 1 might allow read-only operations (DID reading, DTC retrieval), Level 2 allows calibration modification, and Level 3 allows firmware reprogramming. The SecurityAccessLevel configuration in the diagnostic stack maps each UDS service and sub-function to its required authentication level. This mapping must be reviewed against the ISO 21434 threat analysis — every diagnostic service that could cause a safety or security impact needs appropriate protection.

Secure Diagnostic Session Management. Diagnostic sessions have security-relevant lifecycle properties. Authenticated sessions should enforce timeouts (S3Server timer) to prevent indefinite access from an abandoned connection. Session transition from extendedDiagnosticSession to programmingSession should re-authenticate to prevent session escalation attacks. Upon ECU reset or communication loss, all authentication state must be cleared. Diagnostic communication over networked interfaces (DoIP, SOVD) requires TLS transport-layer protection in addition to UDS-level authentication.

Threat Landscape and Mitigations. Common diagnostic attack vectors include: firmware extraction via ReadMemoryByAddress, odometer manipulation via WriteDataByIdentifier, safety system disabling via InputOutputControlByIdentifier, and calibration theft via TransferData. Post-production diagnostic access through OBD-II ports is a specific R155 concern (Annex 5, 7.3.4). Mitigations include: strong authentication, service whitelisting per session type, diagnostic event logging to IdsM for forensic analysis, and physical-access-only restrictions for safety-critical programming operations.

clauses.title: End-to-end protection patterns
clauses.guidance:
End-to-End (E2E) Protection in AUTOSAR provides data integrity and liveness monitoring for communication between software components across ECU boundaries. While originally designed for functional safety (ISO 26262), E2E protection overlaps with cybersecurity requirements and plays a complementary role to SecOC in the vehicle's defense-in-depth strategy.

E2E Profiles and Mechanisms. AUTOSAR defines multiple E2E profiles (Profile 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 11, 22, 44) with varying protection characteristics. Each profile specifies a CRC polynomial and width, a counter mechanism for sequence monitoring, and optional data ID for source identification. Profile 11 uses CRC-8 with an 8-bit counter for short messages. Profile 22 uses CRC-8 with a 4-bit counter, common for CAN signals. Profile 44 uses CRC-32 for Ethernet-based communication where bandwidth is less constrained. The E2E transformer automatically prepends or appends the protection header to the serialized signal group, and the receiver-side transformer verifies integrity and monitors counter continuity.

Security vs. Safety Scope. E2E protection and SecOC serve different but complementary purposes. E2E protects against random communication errors, signal corruption, message loss, and stuck-at faults — these are safety concerns under ISO 26262 ASIL requirements. SecOC protects against deliberate attacks: spoofing, replay, and injection by a malicious actor. A CRC (used by E2E) provides no protection against an attacker who can compute the correct CRC for a crafted message. However, E2E counter monitoring does provide limited security value — it detects message injection that disrupts the expected counter sequence, which can trigger an alert even without cryptographic authentication.

Configuration Strategy. For safety-critical communication paths (ASIL B and above), E2E protection is mandatory per ISO 26262 Part 6. For security-sensitive paths, SecOC is the primary control. Many communication paths require both: E2E for safety and SecOC for security. The combined overhead (E2E header + SecOC authenticator) consumes additional bus bandwidth, which must be accounted for in the communication matrix design. On bandwidth-constrained CAN networks, the combined overhead can be significant — a standard 8-byte CAN frame may have only 2-3 bytes remaining for actual signal data after E2E and SecOC headers.

Integration with Security Monitoring. E2E protection status (E2E_P_OK, E2E_P_REPEATED, E2E_P_WRONG_SEQUENCE, E2E_P_ERROR) should be forwarded to IdsM as security-relevant events when they indicate potential attack patterns rather than random faults. A sustained E2E_P_WRONG_SEQUENCE condition on a CAN message could indicate an injection attack. Correlating E2E failures with SecOC verification status on the same communication path provides higher confidence in distinguishing attacks from transient faults.

mappings.notes: E2E patterns intersect safety and security at system level.

clauses.title: Automotive Ethernet Security
clauses.guidance:
Automotive Ethernet security in AUTOSAR addresses the protection of high-bandwidth in-vehicle networks that carry SOME/IP services, diagnostics over IP (DoIP), time synchronization, and multimedia streaming. Unlike CAN, Ethernet's higher bandwidth and IP-based protocol stack exposes vehicles to network-layer attacks familiar from IT environments, requiring layered security controls.

Layer 2: MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE). MACsec provides hop-by-hop encryption and authentication at the Ethernet frame level. Each MACsec-capable switch port encrypts frames using AES-GCM-128 or AES-GCM-256, preventing eavesdropping and frame manipulation on physical links. MACsec is particularly valuable for backbone connections between domain controllers where sensitive data transits multiple switch hops. The key distribution for MACsec uses MKA (MACsec Key Agreement) protocol, which must integrate with the AUTOSAR KeyM module for key lifecycle management. MACsec adds 16-32 bytes of overhead per frame and introduces latency at each switch hop — both factors must be considered in the vehicle's timing analysis.

Layer 3: IPsec. IPsec provides end-to-end encryption and authentication for IP traffic between ECUs. In transport mode, IPsec protects the payload of IP packets while leaving headers visible for routing. In tunnel mode, it encapsulates the entire original packet. IPsec with ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) using AES-GCM provides both confidentiality and integrity. For automotive use, IPsec is relevant for protecting communication between ECUs in different network segments that traverses intermediate gateways. The IKE (Internet Key Exchange) protocol negotiates security associations and keys, requiring integration with the Crypto Stack.

Layer 4+: TLS for Application Protocols. TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 protect TCP-based communication including diagnostic communication (DoIP with TLS), SOME/IP service calls requiring confidentiality, and connections to external endpoints (telematics, OTA servers). AUTOSAR provides TLS integration through the TcpIp module with crypto operations delegated to CSM. Certificate management for TLS endpoints uses KeyM for storage and validation of server and client certificates. TLS 1.3 is preferred for new designs due to reduced handshake latency and removal of weak cipher suites.

Network Segmentation and Firewalling. Automotive Ethernet networks should be segmented using VLANs (IEEE 802.1Q) to isolate trust domains — separating safety-critical powertrain communication from infotainment, telematics from chassis control. AUTOSAR's EthIfFirewall module implements packet filtering rules at each ECU's Ethernet interface, enforcing policies on source/destination addresses, ports, and protocols. Firewall rules must be defined per the vehicle's security architecture and updated through authenticated mechanisms only. Cross-domain communication requires explicit firewall allow rules and should pass through gateway ECUs that apply additional security checks.

R155 Relevance. Automotive Ethernet security controls address multiple R155 threats: unauthorized network access (7.3.4), data interception (7.3.4), and lateral movement within the vehicle network. Network segmentation design and firewall rule sets are standard type approval evidence items.

clauses.title: In-vehicle firewall policy model
clauses.guidance:
In-vehicle firewall policy in AUTOSAR defines the filtering rules and governance model for controlling network traffic between ECUs, network segments, and trust domains. Unlike enterprise firewalls that evolve through operational rule changes, automotive firewall policies are defined at design time, validated during integration testing, and remain static through the vehicle lifecycle unless updated through authenticated software updates.

Policy Model Structure. The AUTOSAR firewall policy model operates at multiple network layers. At Layer 2, MAC address filtering controls which Ethernet frames are accepted by each ECU interface. At Layer 3, IP address and subnet filtering isolates network segments. At Layer 4, port-based filtering restricts which services are reachable on each ECU. The EthIfFirewall module implements these rules as ordered filter lists — packets are evaluated against rules sequentially, with the first matching rule determining the action (ACCEPT, DROP, LOG_AND_DROP). Default policy should be deny-all, with explicit allow rules for each authorized communication path defined in the vehicle communication matrix.

Lifecycle Governance. Firewall policies transition through defined lifecycle stages: design (derived from security architecture and TARA), implementation (configured in ARXML), validation (tested during system integration), production (flashed to ECU), and maintenance (updated via OTA if threats change). Each stage should include security review: design reviews verify policy completeness against the communication matrix, integration testing verifies that legitimate traffic passes and unauthorized traffic is blocked, and production audits verify that development exceptions have been removed. Policy changes in the field require the same authentication and authorization as firmware updates.

Gateway Firewall Considerations. Central gateway ECUs implement the most critical firewall policies because they control traffic between network domains. A gateway connecting the OBD-II diagnostic segment to the powertrain CAN domain must enforce strict filtering to prevent diagnostic interface abuse from reaching safety-critical ECUs. Gateway policies must account for legitimate cross-domain communication (diagnostic routing, network management, time synchronization) while blocking unauthorized flows. Stateful inspection — tracking connection state for TCP sessions — adds security at the cost of memory and processing overhead, and may be warranted for DoIP connections through the gateway.

R155 and ISO 21434 Mapping. Firewall policies are direct implementations of the network segmentation controls identified in TARA risk treatments. R155 Annex 5 threat 7.3.4 covers measures to protect vehicle networks against unauthorized access, and a well-documented firewall policy with test evidence is a standard type approval work product. The policy documentation should map each rule to the communication path it authorizes and the threat it mitigates.

clauses.title: Intrusion Detection System Manager
clauses.guidance:
IdsM (Intrusion Detection System Manager) is the AUTOSAR module that coordinates security event collection, qualification, and reporting across the vehicle ECU network. It acts as the central aggregation point for security-relevant events generated by other BSW modules and application software, forwarding qualified events to an external Vehicle Security Operations Center (VSOC) for analysis and response.

Security Event Collection. IdsM collects security events from multiple sources within the ECU and across the vehicle network. Event sources include: SecOC MAC verification failures (indicating message authentication attacks or configuration errors), anomalous CAN message patterns (unexpected message IDs, altered cycle times, bus-off events), diagnostic service abuse (repeated SecurityAccess failures, unauthorized service requests), secure boot verification failures, firewall rule violations, TLS handshake failures, and application-level anomaly detections. Each event source reports to IdsM through the IdsM_SetSecurityEvent interface, providing an event ID, timestamp, and optional context data. IdsM maintains per-source event buffers to handle burst conditions without losing events.

CAN Anomaly Detection. For CAN networks, IdsM integrates with network monitoring functions that detect protocol-level and traffic-level anomalies. Protocol-level detection identifies violations of the CAN specification (illegal bit sequences, error frame floods, bus-off attacks). Traffic-level anomaly detection monitors message frequencies and flags deviations from the expected communication matrix — for example, a CAN ID that normally transmits at 100ms intervals suddenly appearing at 10ms intervals, or a CAN ID appearing that is not defined in the DBC/ARXML communication description. Statistical anomaly detection compares observed traffic patterns against learned baselines, flagging deviations exceeding configurable thresholds. This detection is particularly important for identifying ECU compromise, where an attacker has gained control of a node and begins injecting messages.

Event Qualification. Not every detected event warrants external reporting. IdsM implements a qualification pipeline that classifies events as qualified (confirmed security-relevant, must be reported) or non-qualified (informational, logged locally). Qualification criteria include event severity, event frequency (a single SecOC failure may be noise; a burst of failures from the same source is an attack indicator), correlation with other events (simultaneous failures across multiple ECUs suggest a network-level attack), and configurable thresholds. The IdsM Security Event Definition (SED) configuration specifies qualification rules per event type. The Default Error Tracer (DET) integration allows IdsM to also receive error reports from the standard AUTOSAR error handling infrastructure, correlating software faults with potential security incidents.

Event Reporting Pipeline. Qualified events are packaged into IDS report PDUs and transmitted to the gateway ECU or telematics control unit for forwarding to the VSOC. The reporting pipeline includes bandwidth throttling to prevent IDS traffic from consuming excessive network bandwidth — this is configured through rate-limiting parameters per event category and a global reporting rate cap. Report PDUs are structured according to the vehicle manufacturer's VSOC interface specification, typically using a standardized event format that includes event type, source ECU identifier, timestamp, severity classification, and relevant context data. Reports are authenticated using SecOC to prevent an attacker from spoofing or suppressing IDS alerts.

Integration with External SIEM. Beyond the vehicle-side IdsM, the complete IDS architecture extends to backend infrastructure. IdsM reports reach the VSOC through the telematics channel (typically via the OEM's connected vehicle platform). The VSOC ingests events into a SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system where they are correlated across the fleet — a single event on one vehicle may be insignificant, but the same event pattern across hundreds of vehicles indicates a coordinated attack. Fleet-level analytics identify attack campaigns, vulnerable software versions, and geographic patterns. The feedback loop from VSOC to vehicle enables response actions: updated IDS rules pushed via OTA, targeted software patches, or in severe cases, fleet-wide alerts.

R155 Compliance. IdsM directly supports R155 Article 7.2.2.2(g), which requires the vehicle type to have capabilities for monitoring and detecting cyber attacks. The IDS configuration, event taxonomy, reporting architecture, and VSOC integration plan are key CSMS work products for type approval. Demonstrating detection coverage against the vehicle-specific threat catalog (TARA output) is a common type approval evidence requirement.

clauses.title: IDS rule and event strategy
clauses.guidance:
IDS (Intrusion Detection System) rule strategy in AUTOSAR defines the detection logic, event taxonomy, and alert management approach that determine how effectively a vehicle identifies and reports security incidents. The rule set must balance detection coverage against alert quality — too many false positives overwhelm the Vehicle Security Operations Center (VSOC), while too few rules leave attack vectors unmonitored.

Rule Architecture. IDS rules are organized into detection categories aligned with the vehicle's threat model. Network-level rules monitor CAN bus traffic patterns, Ethernet traffic flows, and protocol compliance. Host-level rules monitor ECU behavior: unexpected software execution, memory access violations, resource consumption anomalies, and diagnostic service patterns. Application-level rules monitor domain-specific behavior: unusual calibration access, abnormal sensor readings that could indicate sensor spoofing, and unexpected state transitions. Each rule specifies a detection condition, severity classification, and reporting requirements. Rules should map directly to specific threats from the vehicle TARA — this mapping provides traceability from threat identification through detection capability.

Detection Coverage Strategy. The rule set should cover the vehicle's prioritized threat catalog with detection rules for each threat rated medium or above in the TARA. Coverage analysis identifies gaps where threats exist but no detection rule monitors for them. For each attack vector, consider whether detection is possible at the network layer (anomalous traffic patterns), the host layer (ECU behavior deviation), or the application layer (domain-specific indicators). Some threats may require multi-layer detection: a CAN injection attack might be detected by traffic frequency anomaly (network layer) and by E2E counter discontinuity (host layer) and by implausible control signal values (application layer). Defense in depth applies to detection as well as prevention.

Alert Quality Management. Rule tuning reduces false positives while maintaining detection sensitivity. Each rule should have a defined baseline from normal vehicle operation — established during vehicle validation testing across representative driving conditions. Threshold parameters should be calibrated to flag genuine anomalies without triggering on normal operational variations (e.g., CAN bus load increases during active driving vs. parking). Alert aggregation combines multiple related low-severity events into a single higher-severity alert, reducing VSOC workload. Alert suppression rules prevent known-benign conditions (such as communication interruptions during ECU sleep/wake transitions) from generating false alerts.

R155 Evidence. The IDS rule set and its mapping to the vehicle threat catalog is a key R155 work product. Type approval authorities may ask to see detection coverage analysis, false positive rates from validation testing, and the VSOC alert handling procedures. ISO 21434 Clause 8 (continual cybersecurity activities) requires ongoing monitoring, and the IDS rule set is the technical implementation of that requirement.

mappings.notes: IDS telemetry supports continual cybersecurity activities.

clauses.title: Key Manager
clauses.guidance:
KeyM (Key Manager) is the AUTOSAR module responsible for cryptographic key lifecycle management, certificate handling, and key provisioning. It coordinates key material flow between external sources (diagnostic tools, OTA servers, manufacturing infrastructure) and the Crypto Stack, ensuring keys are properly stored, validated, and accessible to consuming modules.

Certificate Chain Validation. KeyM implements X.509 certificate chain validation for asymmetric key operations. When a new certificate is presented (during TLS handshake, code signing verification, or diagnostic authentication), KeyM verifies the full chain from the end-entity certificate through intermediate CAs to the trusted root certificate stored in the ECU. The validation process checks signature integrity, validity period (notBefore/notAfter), key usage extensions, and basic constraints. KeyM uses the CSM for the actual signature verification operations but manages the chain traversal logic and trust anchor storage. Root certificates are provisioned during manufacturing and stored in tamper-protected HSM memory. Certificate revocation checking is limited in automotive — CRL (Certificate Revocation List) distribution is impractical for vehicles with intermittent connectivity, so short-lived certificates with frequent rotation are preferred over long-lived certificates with revocation infrastructure.

Certificate Installation Flow. New certificates are installed through a defined sequence: the requester (diagnostic service or OTA client) submits the certificate to KeyM via Csm_CertificateParse, KeyM validates the chain as described above, and upon successful validation, stores the certificate and associated public key in the configured key slot. The installation can be triggered through UDS service 0x29 (Authentication) sub-function certificateExchange or through a proprietary mechanism. KeyM tracks installation state through the KeyM_CertificateStatus type, which transitions through states: NOT_AVAILABLE, SUBMITTED, VALIDATED, INSTALLED, REVOKED.

Key Provisioning Approaches. Key provisioning occurs in two primary contexts: manufacturing line provisioning and field provisioning (OTA). Manufacturing provisioning injects the initial key hierarchy during ECU production — typically the Hardware Unique Key (HUK), root certificates, initial CMAC keys for SecOC, and diagnostic authentication keys. This happens through a secure manufacturing station connected via UDS, with the ECU in a special provisioning mode protected by physical access controls. Field provisioning handles key rotation and update during the vehicle lifetime, using authenticated OTA channels with end-to-end encryption. Both paths converge on the Csm_KeyElementSet() interface, but the authentication and authorization mechanisms differ.

HSM Key Slot Management. KeyM manages the mapping between logical key identifiers (used by CSM jobs) and physical HSM key slots. HSM devices have a limited number of slots (typically 16-128 depending on the HSM variant), and slot allocation must be planned during the security architecture phase. Each slot has access attributes: some are write-once (root keys), some are updatable (session keys), and some are volatile (derived keys that exist only during a power cycle). KeyM tracks slot occupancy and enforces slot-type constraints. A common design mistake is exhausting HSM slots by allocating one per SecOC key — instead, a key hierarchy should use HSM slots only for master keys and derive working keys into protected RAM.

Certificate Format and AUTOSAR Specifics. AUTOSAR defines a certificate format based on X.509v3 but with automotive-specific extensions. The format includes fields for ECU identification (VIN binding), intended purpose (code signing, TLS, diagnostic auth), and validity scoping (per-domain or vehicle-wide). The certificate storage format in NVM follows the AUTOSAR Certificate Data Type definition, which packages the DER-encoded certificate with metadata for KeyM management.

ISO 21434 and R155 Relevance. Key management is addressed by ISO 21434 Clause 10.4.2 (cybersecurity specifications for key management) and directly supports R155 Annex 5 threat 7.3.8 (measures to protect cryptographic key material). The key hierarchy design document, key provisioning procedures, and key rotation plan should be included as CSMS work products. HSM key protection level is a common question in type approval technical assessments.

clauses.title: Key provisioning lifecycle
clauses.guidance:
Key provisioning lifecycle in AUTOSAR covers the end-to-end process of generating, distributing, installing, rotating, and retiring cryptographic keys used by vehicle ECUs. Proper key lifecycle management is a foundational security control — compromised or poorly managed keys undermine all cryptographic protections built on top of them.

Manufacturing Line Provisioning. Initial key injection occurs during ECU manufacturing on a secured production line. The provisioning station connects to the ECU via UDS diagnostic interface, authenticates using a manufacturing-specific credential (typically derived from a factory master key), and injects the key hierarchy: Hardware Unique Key (HUK) into HSM OTP storage, root certificates into protected NVM, initial SecOC symmetric keys, diagnostic authentication keys, and TLS identity certificates. The manufacturing station itself must be secured — connected to a Key Management System (KMS) that generates keys, logs all provisioning events, and enforces access control. The provisioning process must be designed to prevent key material from appearing in cleartext on unprotected systems. For HSM-equipped ECUs, key injection uses the HSM's secure import protocol that encrypts key material in transit and decrypts it only inside the HSM boundary.

Field Provisioning (OTA Key Update). Keys must be rotatable during the vehicle lifetime to address key compromise, algorithm migration, or certificate expiration. OTA key updates use authenticated and encrypted channels — the OTA server authenticates to the ECU using existing credentials, negotiates a secure session, and delivers new key material. The update sequence must be atomic: either all related keys are updated successfully, or the ECU rolls back to the previous key set. Partial key updates (e.g., updating the sender's SecOC key but not the receiver's) break authenticated communication. Key update coordination across multiple ECUs requires a vehicle-level key distribution protocol, often orchestrated by the gateway or central compute platform.

Key Retirement and Revocation. When keys reach end-of-life (due to time-based policy, suspected compromise, or algorithm deprecation), they must be securely erased from all storage locations. HSM key slots support secure erase operations that overwrite key material. NVM-stored keys require explicit zeroization. Certificate revocation is communicated through updated certificate status information distributed via OTA. After key retirement, the system must verify that no module still references the retired key — dangling key references cause authentication failures.

ISO 21434 and R155 Mapping. Key provisioning procedures are ISO 21434 Clause 10 work products and support R155 Annex 5 threat 7.3.8. The key management plan — documenting key types, protection levels, rotation schedules, and provisioning procedures — is a standard type approval evidence item. Manufacturing key injection security is often audited as part of the production CSMS assessment.

mappings.notes: Update package lifecycle should account for key lifecycle dependencies.

clauses.title: PDU router security considerations
clauses.guidance:
PDU Router security in AUTOSAR addresses security-relevant aspects of message routing between different network interfaces and communication stacks within an ECU. The PduR module routes PDUs between upper layers (COM, DCM, SecOC) and lower layers (CanIf, FrIf, EthIf, LinIf), and its routing configuration directly affects which messages are exposed to which network segments.

Message Exposure Minimization. A core security principle for PDU routing is that messages should only be forwarded to network segments where they are needed. A PDU containing safety-critical powertrain data should not be routed to the infotainment Ethernet segment even if the routing path exists. The PduR routing table defines exactly which PDUs are forwarded between which interfaces, and this table should be reviewed against the vehicle's security architecture to verify that no unnecessary cross-domain message exposure exists. Gateway ECUs are particularly sensitive — their routing tables determine the information flow between trust domains, and an overly permissive routing configuration effectively bypasses network segmentation.

Routing and SecOC Interaction. The PduR routing path determines where SecOC processing occurs in the message flow. For gateway routing (PDU received on one interface and forwarded to another), the gateway must decide whether to verify-then-re-authenticate (removing the sender's MAC, verifying it, and generating a new MAC for the destination segment) or to pass through the Secured I-PDU unchanged. Verify-then-re-authenticate provides per-segment authentication with independent keys, while pass-through is simpler but requires shared keys across segments. The choice depends on the security architecture — per-segment keys provide better isolation but increase key management complexity.

Routing Table Integrity. The PduR routing configuration is a security-sensitive parameter set. Unauthorized modification of routing tables (through compromised calibration tools or exploited diagnostic access) could redirect, suppress, or duplicate messages in ways that compromise both safety and security. Routing configuration should be protected by the same integrity verification mechanisms used for other security-critical calibration data — typically as part of the secure boot verification scope and protected against unauthorized write access through diagnostic access control.

Gateway Routing Performance. Security-related routing operations (SecOC verify/re-authenticate, firewall rule evaluation) add latency to the message forwarding path. For time-critical PDUs routed through gateway ECUs, the total routing delay including security processing must be verified against the communication timing requirements. AUTOSAR configuration tools provide timing analysis capabilities, but security overhead must be explicitly included in the timing model.

clauses.title: Secure Onboard Communication
clauses.guidance:
SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication) is the AUTOSAR module responsible for authenticating PDUs (Protocol Data Units) on CAN, CAN FD, FlexRay, and Ethernet networks. It protects against message spoofing, replay attacks, and unauthorized injection by appending a truncated Message Authentication Code (MAC) and a freshness value to each secured PDU.

MAC Generation and Verification. SecOC uses CMAC-AES-128 as the default MAC algorithm, though the architecture supports other algorithms via the Crypto Service Manager (CSM) interface. The sender invokes SecOC_Transmit, which triggers MAC computation over the authentic I-PDU data concatenated with the freshness value. On the receiver side, SecOC verifies the MAC by recomputing it from the received data and comparing against the truncated MAC in the Secured I-PDU. The verification result is reported through the SecOC_VerifyStatus callback to upper layers. A failed verification must trigger a security event to the IdsM and may suppress forwarding of the PDU to consuming SWCs.

Freshness Value Management. The freshness value is critical for replay protection. AUTOSAR defines a composite freshness value typically consisting of three components: a trip counter (incremented each ignition cycle), a message counter (incremented per transmission), and a reset counter (incremented on counter synchronization reset). The freshness value manager (FVM) is a separate module that provides freshness values through the SecOC_GetTxFreshness and SecOC_GetRxFreshness interfaces. Only a truncated portion of the freshness value is transmitted on the bus to conserve bandwidth, configured via the SecOCFreshnessValueTruncLength parameter. The receiver reconstructs the full freshness value from the truncated portion using local state. This reconstruction requires synchronization between sender and receiver, which is handled through a freshness value synchronization protocol — typically a challenge-response exchange at startup or after communication loss.

Secured I-PDU Structure. The Secured I-PDU contains three fields: the Authentic I-PDU (original payload), the truncated freshness value, and the truncated MAC. The layout and lengths of each field are configured per security profile. The SecOCAuthInfoTruncLength parameter controls the total number of bits allocated for authentication information. Typical configurations allocate 24-64 bits for the truncated MAC depending on the required security level — safety-critical powertraining messages often use longer MACs than comfort features. The truncation length directly affects both security strength and bus bandwidth consumption, so it must be selected based on a risk assessment aligned with ISO 21434 TARA results.

Crypto Stack Integration. SecOC does not implement cryptographic primitives directly. It delegates MAC computation and verification to the CSM through standardized job-based APIs. The CSM routes these requests to the appropriate crypto driver — either a software implementation (Crypto_30) or a hardware security module (HSM) via CryIf. Key material is referenced by CSM key element IDs, not raw key bytes. The symmetric keys used for CMAC must be provisioned through the KeyM module and stored in HSM-protected key slots for production configurations. Key rotation requires updating both sender and receiver keys atomically, which is typically coordinated through diagnostic services or OTA update mechanisms.

Configuration Considerations. Each SecOC security profile defines: the authentication algorithm (via CSM job reference), freshness value source, truncation lengths for MAC and freshness value, verification retry count, and override status for development versus production modes. The SecOCOverrideStatusIndication parameter allows disabling authentication during development but must be locked out in production builds. The number of verification retries (SecOCVerificationRetryCount) should be limited to prevent timing side-channel attacks.

Diagnostic and Development Considerations. During integration testing, SecOC authentication failures are a common source of communication breakdowns. Typical root causes include mismatched key material between sender and receiver, freshness value desynchronization after partial startup sequences, and incorrect truncation length configuration. Production diagnostics should expose SecOC verification failure counters per PDU through routine-control DIDs. R155 Annex 5 Part A threat 7.3.4 specifically addresses CAN message authentication, making SecOC a primary technical control for type approval evidence. The SecOC configuration and its mapping to the vehicle threat analysis should be documented as part of the CSMS work products submitted to the type approval authority.

clauses.title: Secure Boot
clauses.guidance:
Secure Boot in the AUTOSAR context establishes a chain of trust that verifies the authenticity and integrity of all software executed on an ECU, from the initial hardware reset through application startup. It prevents execution of unauthorized, modified, or rolled-back firmware — a primary defense against code injection and persistent compromise attacks.

Chain of Trust Architecture. The boot chain consists of sequential verification stages. The Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT) is an immutable component — typically a masked ROM bootloader or a dedicated secure element — that contains the initial verification logic and the root public key. This component cannot be updated and forms the trust anchor for the entire chain. The first-stage bootloader, verified by the HRoT, loads and verifies the second-stage bootloader. The second-stage bootloader verifies the application software, calibration data, and configuration blocks. Each stage computes a cryptographic signature (RSA-2048, RSA-3072, or ECDSA-P256) over the next stage's image and compares it against a stored reference signature. Only if verification succeeds does execution transfer to the next stage. The signature algorithm and key length must be selected to provide adequate security margin for the expected vehicle lifetime (15-20 years) — ECDSA-P256 or RSA-3072 minimum for new designs.

Measured Boot and Attestation. Beyond simple pass/fail verification, measured boot extends the chain of trust by recording hash measurements of each boot stage into a secure log. These measurements can be stored in HSM-protected Platform Configuration Registers (PCRs) or equivalent structures. Remote attestation allows a backend server to request the boot measurement log and verify that the ECU is running expected software versions. Attestation is particularly valuable for fleet monitoring — the OEM backend can detect ECUs running non-genuine software without requiring physical access. The measurement log should include: bootloader version hash, application software version hash, calibration data hash, and security configuration hash.

Rollback Protection. Secure boot must prevent rollback attacks where an attacker installs an older, vulnerable firmware version that passes signature verification (because it was legitimately signed). Rollback protection uses a monotonic counter stored in one-time-programmable (OTP) memory or an HSM-protected counter. Each firmware image includes a version number that must be greater than or equal to the counter value. After successful boot with a new version, the counter is incremented, permanently preventing boot of older versions. The counter mechanism must be carefully designed — a premature counter increment before the new firmware is fully validated can brick the ECU. Two-phase commit approaches address this: the counter increments only after the new firmware has successfully booted and passed self-tests.

Boot Failure Handling. When verification fails at any stage, the ECU must enter a defined safe state rather than proceeding with potentially compromised software. The safe state behavior depends on the ECU's safety classification: a non-safety-relevant ECU may simply halt and report an error via diagnostic trouble code, while a safety-relevant ECU must maintain minimum functionality per its ISO 26262 safety concept. Failure events should be logged in tamper-resistant storage for forensic analysis and reported to IdsM. A recovery mode should allow re-flashing via diagnostic interface (UDS 0x34/0x36) with additional authentication requirements to prevent an attacker from using recovery mode as a bypass path.

Relationship to HSM. The HSM plays a central role in secure boot: it stores the root public key (and intermediate keys), performs signature verification operations within the secure boundary, manages the rollback counter in protected storage, and provides the Platform Configuration Registers for measured boot. An ECU without HSM can implement software-only secure boot using keys stored in protected flash, but this provides weaker protection since the key material is accessible to an attacker with physical flash access. For R155 type approval, the security architecture documentation should clearly state the HSM protection level for boot verification keys and counter storage.

R155 and ISO 21434 Relevance. Secure boot addresses R155 Annex 5 threat 7.3.4 (measures to prevent unauthorized software modification) and is a standard expectation in type approval technical assessments. ISO 21434 Clause 10 cybersecurity specifications should include the boot chain design, verification algorithm selection rationale, rollback protection mechanism, and failure handling behavior as documented work products.

clauses.title: Secure diagnostics gateway patterns
clauses.guidance:
Secure gateway patterns in AUTOSAR define the security architecture for ECUs that bridge trust domains in segmented and zonal E/E architectures. Gateway ECUs occupy a privileged position in the vehicle network — they control information flow between domains and represent high-value targets for attackers seeking lateral movement.

Domain Segmentation Architecture. The gateway implements the physical and logical boundaries between network domains. In traditional domain architectures, a central gateway connects powertrain CAN, chassis CAN, body CAN, infotainment Ethernet, and the diagnostic (OBD-II) interface. In zonal architectures, zone controllers replace the central gateway with distributed gateways that connect local sensors and actuators to the vehicle backbone. Each gateway enforces security policies at the domain boundary: firewall filtering, SecOC authentication bridging, protocol translation with security context preservation, and rate limiting to prevent denial-of-service propagation across domains.

Diagnostics Gateway Security. Remote diagnostics access (via telematics or connected vehicle platform) passes through gateway ECUs before reaching target ECUs. The gateway must authenticate remote diagnostic sessions, enforce authorization policies (which diagnostic services are permitted remotely vs. requiring physical access), and rate-limit diagnostic traffic to prevent resource exhaustion on target ECUs. SOVD (ISO 17978) introduces service-oriented diagnostic access patterns that require the gateway to enforce authentication at the service API level rather than just at the transport level.

Zonal Architecture Considerations. Zonal E/E architectures distribute gateway functions across multiple zone controllers, each responsible for a physical vehicle zone. This distribution increases the number of trust boundaries and gateway instances that must be secured. Each zone controller must implement independent security policy enforcement — a compromised zone controller should not be able to access resources in other zones. Inter-zone communication over the vehicle backbone should be authenticated (SecOC or TLS depending on the transport), and each zone controller should maintain its own key material rather than sharing keys across zones.

Gateway as Security Monitor. Beyond policy enforcement, gateways are optimal locations for security monitoring because they observe cross-domain traffic. Gateway-based IDS rules can detect unauthorized cross-domain communication, traffic pattern anomalies, and policy violations. Gateway security events should be reported to IdsM with domain context information that helps the VSOC understand the attack's scope and progression. Gateway compromise is a high-impact scenario that should be addressed in the vehicle threat model with specific detection and containment measures.

mappings.notes: Remote diagnostics exposure requires secure gateway architecture.

clauses.title: SOME/IP security controls
clauses.guidance:
SOME/IP (Scalable service-Oriented MiddlewarE over IP) security controls in AUTOSAR address the protection of service-oriented communication on automotive Ethernet networks. SOME/IP enables method calls, event notifications, and field access between ECUs, replacing signal-based communication with a service-oriented model that introduces new attack surfaces.

SOME/IP Protocol Security Risks. SOME/IP was designed for performance and determinism, not security — the base protocol includes no authentication, authorization, or encryption. Messages are transmitted in cleartext with no integrity protection, meaning any ECU on the same network segment can observe, modify, or inject SOME/IP messages. Service Discovery (SOME/IP-SD) broadcasts introduce additional risk: an attacker can offer malicious service instances, subscribe to events containing sensitive data, or suppress legitimate service offers to cause denial of service. These risks are acceptable only within well-segmented, physically protected network domains.

Authentication and Authorization Controls. AUTOSAR provides several mechanisms to secure SOME/IP communication. At the transport layer, TLS wraps SOME/IP-over-TCP connections with encryption and mutual authentication. At the application layer, service-level authentication profiles define per-service authentication requirements — each service can specify whether callers must authenticate and what credentials are accepted. The ara::com security extensions in AUTOSAR Adaptive provide API-level access control, where the platform enforces that only authorized applications can invoke specific service methods or subscribe to events.

Service Discovery Protection. SOME/IP-SD protection requires controlling which ECUs can offer and consume services. Network segmentation (VLANs) limits SD broadcast domains to authorized participants. SD filter rules on each ECU restrict which service offers and subscriptions are processed. In secured configurations, SD messages should be authenticated to prevent service impersonation — an attacker offering a fake brake control service instance could intercept safety-critical method calls.

Integration with Crypto Stack. SOME/IP security controls depend on the AUTOSAR Crypto Stack for TLS operations, MAC computation for application-layer authentication, and key management for session key negotiation. Service authentication credentials (certificates, pre-shared keys) are managed through KeyM and stored in CSM key elements. The performance impact of cryptographic operations on SOME/IP latency must be evaluated — real-time services with sub-millisecond latency requirements may not tolerate TLS overhead and require alternative protection (network isolation, MACsec at Layer 2).

R155 Relevance. SOME/IP security is relevant to R155 Annex 5 threats covering internal vehicle communication protection (7.3.4) and unauthorized service access. The security architecture should document which SOME/IP services are security-critical, what protection mechanisms are applied to each, and the rationale for any services left unprotected.

mappings.notes: Service/Ethernet security concerns are shared.

clauses.title: Chinese Automotive Cybersecurity Standards Overview
clauses.guidance: China has developed mandatory cybersecurity standards for vehicles sold in the Chinese market. Key standards: (1) GB/T 40857-2021 - Vehicle cybersecurity general technical requirements; (2) GB/T 40856-2021 - Software update engineering requirements; (3) GB/T 40855-2021 - Vehicle gateway security requirements; (4) GB 44495-2024 - Mandatory vehicle data security standard. These standards are heavily influenced by UNECE R155/R156 but adapted for Chinese regulatory context. Compliance is required for China Compulsory Certification (CCC) for connected vehicles. Work with Chinese testing labs (CATARC, CAERI) for certification.

clauses.title: GB/T 40855-2021 Gateway Security Requirements
clauses.guidance: Security requirements for vehicle gateways. Gateway is critical security boundary. Requirements: (1) Access control - control traffic between network domains; (2) Message filtering - whitelist/blacklist for allowed messages; (3) Authentication - verify source of messages; (4) Anomaly detection - identify suspicious traffic patterns; (5) Secure boot - verify gateway firmware integrity; (6) Secure communication - protect inter-domain traffic; (7) Diagnostic protection - secure diagnostic routing. Chinese standards emphasize gateway as primary security control point. Expect detailed testing of gateway filtering rules during certification.

clauses.title: GB/T 40855 Section 6: Update Process Governance
clauses.guidance: Defines update process governance and change control for software updates.

mappings.notes: SUMS process governance alignment.
Update planning governance alignment.

clauses.title: GB/T 40855 Section 7: Update Integrity and Rollback
clauses.guidance: Defines update integrity checks and rollback behavior for safe deployment.

mappings.notes: Supports safe execution and rollback controls.
Rollback validation alignment.

clauses.title: GB/T 40856-2021 Software Update Requirements
clauses.guidance: Requirements for vehicle software update systems (Chinese equivalent to R156). Key requirements: (1) Update management system - organizational processes for managing updates; (2) Secure update mechanism - authentication, integrity verification; (3) Rollback capability - ability to revert failed updates; (4) User notification - inform vehicle owners of updates; (5) Update logging - maintain audit trail; (6) OTA infrastructure security - secure backend systems. Applies to all vehicles capable of software updates. Requires demonstration that updates cannot be manipulated and failed updates don't compromise vehicle safety.

clauses.title: GB/T 40856 Section 7: Security Testing
clauses.guidance: Defines security testing methods for in-vehicle and backend cybersecurity controls.

mappings.notes: Test strategy and validation scope alignment.

clauses.title: GB/T 40856 Section 8: Penetration Assessment
clauses.guidance: Defines penetration assessment and attack-simulation practices for homologation readiness.

mappings.notes: Penetration results support type approval evidence packs.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857-2021 Vehicle Cybersecurity Technical Requirements
clauses.guidance: Core cybersecurity requirements for intelligent connected vehicles. Structure mirrors R155 but with Chinese-specific requirements: (1) Organizational security - establish cybersecurity management system; (2) Vehicle development - threat analysis, security design, testing; (3) Production security - secure manufacturing processes; (4) Post-production - monitoring, incident response, updates; (5) Technical requirements - specific controls for communication, gateway, ECU security. Key difference from R155: more prescriptive technical requirements rather than risk-based approach. Applies to M and N category vehicles with connectivity features.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 5: Organizational Requirements
clauses.guidance: Cybersecurity management system requirements for manufacturers. Requirements: (1) Cybersecurity policy - documented and approved by management; (2) Organizational structure - designated cybersecurity roles; (3) Resource allocation - adequate personnel and tools; (4) Competence requirements - staff training and qualification; (5) Supply chain security - requirements flow-down to suppliers; (6) Continuous improvement - regular review and update of CSMS. Similar to R155 Article 6 CSMS certificate requirements. Chinese authorities may audit CSMS implementation.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 6: Vehicle Development Requirements
clauses.guidance: Security engineering during vehicle development. Key requirements: (1) Threat analysis - identify threats to vehicle systems; (2) Risk assessment - evaluate likelihood and impact; (3) Security concept - define security architecture; (4) Security requirements - derive from risk assessment; (5) Security design - implement controls; (6) Security verification - testing and validation. Requires documented evidence throughout development lifecycle. Testing must cover both functional security and penetration testing. Chinese labs will verify compliance through document review and vehicle testing.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 7: Technical Security Requirements
clauses.guidance: Specific technical controls required for vehicles. Areas covered: (1) External interface security - protect Bluetooth, WiFi, cellular, V2X; (2) Internal network security - gateway protection, network segmentation; (3) ECU security - secure boot, secure storage, secure communication; (4) Data security - encryption, access control, secure deletion; (5) Diagnostic security - authentication, authorization; (6) Update security - secure OTA implementation. More prescriptive than R155 - specifies minimum cryptographic algorithms (SM2/SM3/SM4 Chinese algorithms often required), authentication mechanisms, and network segmentation approaches.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 8: Post-market Monitoring
clauses.guidance: Defines post-market monitoring and vulnerability response expectations.

mappings.notes: Supports continual monitoring and vulnerability management.
Continual cybersecurity activities alignment.

clauses.title: GB 44495-2024 Vehicle Data Security
clauses.guidance: Mandatory standard for vehicle data security (effective 2026). This is a GB (mandatory) not GB/T (recommended) standard. Key requirements: (1) Data classification - categorize data by sensitivity; (2) Personal data protection - consent, minimization, anonymization; (3) Important data protection - data that affects national security, public interest; (4) Cross-border transfer - restrictions on exporting certain data; (5) Data localization - some data must be stored in China; (6) Security assessment - required for important data processing. Critical for connected vehicles: must implement data security controls and may need to establish China data centers. Consult with China data privacy lawyers.

clauses.title: China CCC Certification Process
clauses.guidance: Process for obtaining China Compulsory Certification for connected vehicles. Steps: (1) Select accredited testing lab (CATARC, CAERI, CAQC); (2) Submit application with technical documentation; (3) Factory audit - verify CSMS implementation; (4) Vehicle testing - functional and security testing; (5) Report review - lab submits to CNCA; (6) Certificate issuance - valid for 5 years; (7) Surveillance - annual factory audits. Timeline: 6-12 months from application to certificate. Cost: varies by vehicle type and lab. Tips: engage Chinese partner early, prepare documentation in Chinese, budget for multiple test iterations.

clauses.title: Compliance audit evidence package
clauses.guidance: Defines expected evidence package structure for Chinese cybersecurity compliance reviews.

mappings.notes: Evidence pack structure aligns with cybersecurity case expectations.

clauses.title: Chinese Cryptographic Requirements
clauses.guidance: China requires use of Chinese cryptographic algorithms in certain contexts. Key algorithms: (1) SM2 - elliptic curve cryptography (similar to ECDSA); (2) SM3 - hash function (similar to SHA-256); (3) SM4 - block cipher (similar to AES-128); (4) SM9 - identity-based cryptography. When required: government/public sector applications must use SM algorithms; commercial applications may have flexibility. Vehicle cybersecurity: check with certification body whether SM algorithms are required for your use case. Many implementations support dual-stack (SM + international algorithms). Hardware: look for chips supporting SM algorithms or use software implementations.

clauses.title: Data localization controls
clauses.guidance: Defines China-specific controls for cross-border data handling and localization constraints.

mappings.notes: Jurisdictional constraints intersect regional implementation boundaries.

clauses.title: Chinese Cybersecurity Testing Requirements
clauses.guidance: Testing requirements for GB/T compliance verification. Test categories: (1) Document review - verify CSMS, development process documentation; (2) Functional testing - verify security controls work as designed; (3) Penetration testing - attempt to bypass security controls; (4) Fuzz testing - malformed input testing; (5) Vulnerability scanning - automated vulnerability detection. Chinese labs follow national testing guidelines. Key labs: CATARC (Tianjin), CAERI (Chongqing), China Auto (Shanghai). Prepare test vehicles with debug access enabled. Testing typically takes 2-4 weeks per vehicle type. Plan for retesting if failures found.

clauses.title: Chinese V2X Security Requirements
clauses.guidance: Security requirements for Vehicle-to-Everything communication in China. China uses C-V2X (cellular) technology, not DSRC. Key standards: (1) YD/T 3957 - LTE-V2X security; (2) T/CSAE 53-2020 - V2X PKI certificate policy; (3) T/ITS 0058 - V2X application security. Requirements: (1) PKI-based authentication - vehicles must have valid certificates; (2) Message signing - all V2X messages authenticated; (3) Certificate management - enrollment, update, revocation; (4) Privacy protection - pseudonym certificates. China V2X PKI is managed by designated Certificate Authorities. Foreign OEMs must work with Chinese CA system for V2X deployment.

clauses.title: Overview
clauses.guidance:
ISO 14229 (Unified Diagnostic Services) defines the application-layer diagnostic protocol used by nearly every modern automotive ECU. From a cybersecurity perspective, UDS is one of the most significant attack surfaces in a vehicle because it provides direct read, write, and execution capabilities over the CAN bus, DoIP, and other transport layers. Every service defined in ISO 14229 represents a potential entry point for an attacker who gains access to the vehicle's diagnostic interface, whether through the OBD-II port, a compromised telematics unit, or a connected aftermarket device.

The security model of UDS relies on a layered approach. At the base layer, the diagnostic session (0x10) gates which services are available. Above that, SecurityAccess (0x27) and Authentication (0x29) control whether a client is authorized to invoke privileged services. SecuredDataTransmission (0x84) provides payload-level integrity and confidentiality. This layered model means that a weakness at any single layer can cascade: an attacker who bypasses session control may reach services that assume authentication has already occurred.

ISO 14229-1:2020 introduced the Authentication service (0x29) as a modern replacement for the seed-key mechanism of SecurityAccess (0x27). OEMs should treat this as a migration signal. New ECU designs should implement 0x29 with certificate-based authentication, while continuing to support 0x27 only where backward compatibility is required, and only with hardened seed-key algorithms.

From a regulatory standpoint, UNECE R155 Annex 5 requires OEMs to demonstrate that diagnostic interfaces cannot be exploited to compromise vehicle cybersecurity. ISO 21434 clause 10 (product development at the cybersecurity level) requires that each UDS service be evaluated during the TARA process for its attack feasibility and potential impact. The combination of these regulatory requirements means that every service in this standard must be assessed for cybersecurity risk, not just the obvious security-related services.

Implementors should maintain a diagnostic service allowlist for each ECU, explicitly mapping which services are permitted in which sessions and under which authentication states. Services not required for production operation should be disabled or removed from the ECU firmware entirely. Defense-in-depth measures such as message authentication (SecOC), network segmentation, and intrusion detection complement the service-level controls defined in this standard.

clauses.title: Authentication (0x29)
clauses.guidance:
The Authentication service (0x29) was introduced in ISO 14229-1:2020 as the modern replacement for the legacy SecurityAccess (0x27) seed-key mechanism. It provides certificate-based mutual authentication between diagnostic clients and ECUs, enabling cryptographically strong identity verification that addresses the fundamental weaknesses of symmetric seed-key schemes. For any ECU design starting after 2020, 0x29 should be the primary authentication mechanism, with 0x27 retained only for backward compatibility with legacy tooling under strict deprecation timelines.

The service supports multiple authentication sub-functions, most significantly the APCE (Authentication Protocol with Challenge-response using Extended data) flow. In a typical APCE exchange, the client sends a certificate chain to the ECU, the ECU validates the chain against its trust anchor, and both parties complete a challenge-response handshake. This establishes a session key that can be used for subsequent SecuredDataTransmission (0x84) operations. The protocol supports both unilateral (client authenticates to ECU) and mutual (both parties authenticate) modes.

PKI infrastructure is the backbone of 0x29 implementation. Each OEM must operate or contract a certificate authority hierarchy that issues diagnostic certificates. Short-lived certificates are preferred because they reduce the exposure window if a certificate is compromised. The ECU must store a trust anchor (root CA certificate or its hash) in tamper-resistant storage.  Certificate revocation must be handled through either short certificate lifetimes (hours to days for online testers) or CRL distribution for offline workshop testers. An ECU that cannot verify revocation status should deny authentication rather than assume the certificate is valid.

Hardware security module (HSM) integration is critical for both the ECU side and the tester side. The ECU's private key for mutual authentication must be generated and stored within an HSM or secure element. Key extraction from the ECU must be infeasible. On the tester side, diagnostic tools should use hardware tokens or TPM-backed certificate storage rather than software-only key storage, which is vulnerable to extraction by malware on the diagnostic PC.

Session elevation is a key architectural concept. After successful 0x29 authentication, the ECU grants the client elevated privileges that persist for the duration of the diagnostic session. These privileges should be scoped: a certificate for a workshop technician should grant different access than a certificate for an OEM engineering team. Role-based access control can be encoded in certificate extensions (X.509v3 custom extensions) or in the APCE negotiation parameters. This granularity is a major advantage over 0x27, which typically offers only a binary locked/unlocked state per security level.

The relationship to SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication) matters for in-vehicle deployment. While SecOC authenticates individual CAN frames at the network layer, 0x29 authenticates the diagnostic session at the application layer. Both are needed: SecOC prevents spoofing of normal operational messages, while 0x29 prevents unauthorized diagnostic access. They operate at different layers and are complementary, not redundant.

Common implementation pitfalls include: accepting self-signed certificates during development and failing to enforce chain validation in production; storing the ECU private key in unprotected flash memory; implementing the challenge-response with insufficient randomness in the nonce generation; and failing to bind the authenticated session to a specific transport connection (allowing session hijacking if the attacker can inject messages on the same CAN bus after authentication completes). Each of these represents a CAL-relevant vulnerability under ISO 21434 that must be addressed during the product cybersecurity design phase.

R155 Annex 5 paragraph 7.2.2.2 requires that diagnostic interfaces implement adequate authentication. Deploying 0x29 with a properly managed PKI is currently the strongest available mechanism to satisfy this requirement for new vehicle architectures.

mappings.notes: Supports authenticated diagnostic operations in connected environments.

clauses.title: Communication control (0x28)
clauses.guidance:
The CommunicationControl service (0x28) allows a diagnostic tester to suppress or enable normal communication on specific ECU network interfaces. Typical uses include silencing CAN traffic during reprogramming (to free bandwidth) and disabling NM (Network Management) messages to prevent the ECU from entering sleep mode during extended service procedures. While this service appears operationally benign, it creates significant cybersecurity risks when misused or inadequately protected.

The primary threat is denial of service through communication suppression. An attacker with CAN bus access who can invoke 0x28 without authentication can silence an ECU's normal network participation. In a safety-critical context, this means an ECU might stop transmitting brake status, steering angle, or airbag readiness messages. Other ECUs on the bus that depend on these messages may enter fallback modes, degrade functionality, or trigger diagnostic trouble codes. The cascading effect across a vehicle network can be severe, particularly for ECUs that implement time-critical safety functions.

From an implementation standpoint, CommunicationControl should never be accessible in the Default diagnostic session. It should require at minimum an Extended session, and for safety-critical ECUs, it should require prior SecurityAccess (0x27) or Authentication (0x29) authorization. The ECU should validate the controlType parameter strictly, rejecting any reserved or undefined values rather than defaulting to an enable/disable action.

Network segmentation provides an important layer of defense. If the diagnostic interface is on a gateway-separated segment, an attacker who compromises a single domain (such as infotainment) cannot reach 0x28 on powertrain or chassis ECUs. OEMs should ensure that gateway firewall rules block diagnostic service requests originating from non-diagnostic network segments.

A subtle vulnerability exists with the communicationType sub-parameter, which selects which network interfaces are affected. An implementation that allows suppression of the ECU's entire communication stack (including its security-relevant heartbeat messages) creates a more dangerous attack surface than one that only permits suppression of normal application messages while maintaining safety-relevant transmissions.

ISO 21434 TARA should evaluate CommunicationControl as an attack vector for each ECU that implements it.
