Score ranges map to feasibility levels: 0-9 = high feasibility (attack is easy), 10-13 = medium, 14-19 = low, 20-24 = very low, 25+ = infeasible under current assumptions.

**Scoring guidance.** When scoring elapsed time, consider both the development time (creating the attack tool or technique) and the execution time (performing the attack against a target vehicle). For specialist expertise, consider whether the attacker needs automotive-specific knowledge, cryptographic expertise, hardware reverse engineering skills, or RF engineering capabilities. For knowledge of the item, consider whether system documentation is publicly available, whether the ECU firmware can be extracted and analyzed, and whether the network architecture can be inferred from publicly available diagnostic databases. For window of opportunity, consider whether the attack requires proximity to the vehicle, physical access to a port, or can be performed remotely at any time. For equipment, consider whether the attack can be performed with a laptop and off-the-shelf hardware (CAN interfaces, SDR dongles) or requires expensive laboratory equipment (chip probing stations, signal analyzers).

**CVSS-based method.** As an alternative, map CVSS v3.x exploitability metrics to feasibility levels: remote attack vector with low complexity and no privileges required maps to high feasibility; physical attack vector with high complexity and high privileges maps to very low feasibility. This method is simpler but less granular than the attack potential method.

**Common pitfalls.** Inconsistent scoring across analysts is the most common problem. Two analysts rating the same attack scenario may produce scores differing by 10+ points without calibration. Conduct calibration sessions using reference scenarios. Another failure is scoring based on theoretical difficulty rather than demonstrated capability — if an attack technique has been demonstrated at a security conference, the elapsed time and expertise scores should reflect reproduction effort, not original research effort. Over-relying on the window of opportunity factor (assuming physical access is very difficult) ignores insider threats, service access, and scenarios where an attacker has purchased the same vehicle model.

**Evidence for type approval.** Attack feasibility ratings with factor-level scores are required in [WP-15-06]. Auditors will examine scoring rationale, check for consistency, and compare against known automotive attack capabilities.

clauses.title: Example TARA methods
clauses.guidance:
Annex H provides example approaches for conducting the TARA methodology, offering practical guidance for organizations implementing Clause 15.

**STRIDE per element.** Apply STRIDE analysis to each element in the system data flow diagram: for every process, data store, data flow, and external entity, systematically consider Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, and Elevation of Privilege threats. This method provides thorough coverage but can generate a large number of threat scenarios for complex systems. Manage this by focusing detailed analysis on elements at trust boundaries (where data crosses from one security domain to another) and grouping similar threats across elements.

**Attack tree analysis.** Build attack trees for each cybersecurity goal or high-value asset. The root node represents the attacker's objective, and child nodes represent sub-goals or alternative approaches. Leaf nodes represent atomic attack steps that can be individually assessed for feasibility. Attack trees provide a visual, structured representation that facilitates discussion with stakeholders and can be maintained and updated as the system evolves. Tools like Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool, ThreatGet, and Ansys medini analyze support automated attack tree generation from system models.

**Kill chain analysis.** Adapt the cyber kill chain model for automotive: (1) reconnaissance — gathering information about the vehicle model, ECU firmware versions, and communication protocols; (2) weaponization — developing attack tools and exploits; (3) delivery — establishing a communication channel to the target (wireless, physical, supply chain); (4) exploitation — executing the attack against a vulnerability; (5) installation — establishing persistent access if needed; (6) command and control — maintaining the ability to issue commands to the compromised system; (7) actions on objectives — achieving the attacker's goal. This framework helps identify detection and disruption opportunities at each stage.

**Hybrid approaches.** In practice, most organizations use a combination: STRIDE per element for systematic identification, attack trees for detailed path analysis of high-priority threats, and kill chain analysis for evaluating multi-stage attacks against connected vehicles. The choice of method should be documented in the cybersecurity plan (Clause 6) and applied consistently across the organization.

**Common pitfalls.** Using a single method exclusively creates blind spots — STRIDE may miss multi-stage attack chains, attack trees may miss threats not related to the explicitly modeled goals, and kill chain analysis may not cover all STRIDE categories. Generating threat scenarios without connecting them to the subsequent TARA steps (impact rating, feasibility rating, risk determination) produces analysis that has no actionable outcome. Teams that treat TARA methods as a one-time exercise rather than a living analysis miss threats introduced by design changes, new components, or evolving attacker capabilities.

**Evidence for type approval.** The TARA methodology must be documented and consistently applied. Auditors expect to see the chosen methods, their application to the specific system, and traceability from identified threats through the complete TARA process to risk treatment decisions.

clauses.title: SAE J3061 Overview
clauses.guidance: SAE J3061 (2016) was the first automotive cybersecurity standard, predating ISO 21434. Key concepts: (1) Cybersecurity lifecycle - concept, development, production, operation, decommissioning; (2) Feature-based development - integrates with existing automotive processes; (3) Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (TARA) - originated many concepts later refined in ISO 21434; (4) Guiding principles - defense in depth, least privilege, fail-safe. While superseded by ISO 21434 for new programs, J3061 is still referenced in legacy contracts and provides good introductory material. Many J3061 concepts map directly to ISO 21434.

clauses.title: Supporting Processes
clauses.guidance: Organizational processes supporting cybersecurity. Includes: (1) Cybersecurity governance - organizational structure, responsibilities; (2) Cybersecurity culture - awareness, training; (3) Tool qualification - security of development tools; (4) Supplier management - cybersecurity requirements for suppliers. Similar to ISO 21434 Clauses 5 and 7. J3061 was less prescriptive about organizational requirements than ISO 21434.

clauses.title: Governance and process assurance
clauses.guidance: Defines governance, oversight, and process assurance mechanisms across the cybersecurity lifecycle.

mappings.notes: Governance baseline aligns with organizational cybersecurity management.

clauses.title: Metrics and continuous improvement
clauses.guidance: Defines cybersecurity metric collection and continuous improvement loops for engineering effectiveness.

clauses.title: Guiding Principles
clauses.guidance: Foundational security principles for vehicle cybersecurity. Principles: (1) Defense in depth - multiple layers of security controls; (2) Least privilege - minimal access rights for functions; (3) Separation of domains - isolate critical from non-critical systems; (4) Fail-safe design - secure defaults, graceful degradation; (5) No security by obscurity - don't rely on hidden designs; (6) Simplicity - reduce attack surface through simple designs. These principles remain valid and should guide all automotive security architecture decisions.

clauses.title: Concept Phase
clauses.guidance: Initiates cybersecurity for a vehicle feature. Activities: (1) Item definition - scope, boundaries, interfaces; (2) Cybersecurity goals - high-level security objectives; (3) Preliminary TARA - initial threat identification; (4) Cybersecurity concept - high-level security architecture. Similar to ISO 21434 Clause 9 but with different terminology. J3061 uses 'feature' where ISO 21434 uses 'item'. The outputs feed into detailed design phase.

clauses.title: Development Phase
clauses.guidance: Detailed cybersecurity engineering during product development. Key activities: (1) System-level TARA - detailed threat analysis; (2) Security requirements - derived from TARA and cybersecurity goals; (3) Security architecture - detailed security design; (4) Security implementation - coding standards, secure coding; (5) Security testing - verification and validation. J3061 introduced the concept of security testing levels analogous to ISO 26262 ASIL decomposition.

clauses.title: Production Phase
clauses.guidance: Cybersecurity during manufacturing. Focus areas: (1) Secure manufacturing - protect keys, certificates, software; (2) Secure flashing - authenticated software installation; (3) Production security controls - physical and logical access; (4) End-of-line testing - verify security features work. Similar to ISO 21434 Clause 12 but less detailed. Key concern: protecting cryptographic material during production.

clauses.title: Operation and Maintenance Phase
clauses.guidance: Cybersecurity for vehicles in the field. Key activities: (1) Vulnerability monitoring - track new vulnerabilities; (2) Incident response - detect and respond to attacks; (3) Security updates - patch vulnerabilities; (4) Field monitoring - collect security telemetry. J3061 was early in recognizing the need for post-production security monitoring. Maps to ISO 21434 Clause 8 (continual activities) and Clause 13 (operations).

clauses.title: TARA Method Examples
clauses.guidance: Provides example TARA methods. Methods described: (1) EVITA - uses attack trees and attack potential calculation; (2) HEAVENS - threat modeling with security levels; (3) TVRA - ETSI method adapted for vehicles; (4) Attack trees - hierarchical threat decomposition; (5) Misuse cases - extend use cases with attacker perspective. These methods influenced ISO 21434 Annex H. Choose a method that fits your organization's risk assessment experience.

clauses.title: TISAX Overview and Assessment Levels
clauses.guidance: TISAX (Trusted Information Security Assessment Exchange) is the automotive industry's information security assessment standard, managed by ENX Association. Key concepts: (1) Assessment Levels - AL1 (normal protection, self-assessment), AL2 (high protection, remote assessment), AL3 (very high protection, on-site assessment); (2) Scope Labels - 'Info high' for confidential data, 'Info very high' for strictly confidential, 'Proto' for prototype protection, 'Vehicle' for test vehicles; (3) Validity - 3 years from assessment date; (4) Exchange - Results shared via ENX portal with OEM customers. TISAX is mandatory for suppliers working with VW, BMW, Mercedes, and most German OEMs. Start the process 6-9 months before you need the certificate.

clauses.title: Information Security Policies
clauses.guidance: Requires documented information security policies approved by management. Key requirements: (1) Information security policy - high-level commitment and direction; (2) Topic-specific policies - detailed policies for access control, cryptography, supplier security, etc.; (3) Review cycle - policies must be reviewed at planned intervals or when significant changes occur; (4) Communication - policies must be communicated to employees and relevant external parties. Maps to ISO 27001 Clause 5.2 and ISO 21434 Clause 5. Tip: Your ISO 21434 cybersecurity policy can satisfy TISAX if it covers information security scope.

clauses.title: Supplier Relationships
clauses.guidance: Manages security in supplier relationships. Key requirements: (1) Supplier security policy - requirements for suppliers handling your data; (2) Supply chain security - addressing security through the chain; (3) Supplier agreements - security clauses in contracts; (4) Monitoring and review - ongoing supplier assessment. Critical for automotive tier structure: you must flow down TISAX-equivalent requirements to your sub-suppliers. Document supplier security assessments and maintain approved supplier list.

clauses.title: Incident Management
clauses.guidance: Establishes incident response capabilities. Requirements: (1) Incident management process - detection, reporting, response, recovery; (2) Reporting procedures - internal and external notification; (3) Assessment and decision - severity classification; (4) Response procedures - containment, eradication, recovery; (5) Learning from incidents - post-incident review, improvements. For OEM customer data breaches: know your contractual notification timelines (often 24-72 hours). Maintain incident log and conduct tabletop exercises.

clauses.title: Business Continuity
clauses.guidance: Ensures information security during disruptions. Key elements: (1) Business continuity planning - maintain security during crisis; (2) Redundancy - backup systems for critical functions; (3) Testing - regular BCP testing and updates. For automotive suppliers: consider impact on customer production if your systems fail. Define RTOs (Recovery Time Objectives) aligned with customer requirements. Include cybersecurity scenarios (ransomware) in BCP testing.

clauses.title: Compliance
clauses.guidance: Ensures compliance with legal and contractual requirements. Areas covered: (1) Legal requirements - identify applicable laws (GDPR, export controls); (2) Intellectual property - respect IP rights, license compliance; (3) Privacy - personal data protection; (4) Cryptographic regulations - export/import controls on crypto; (5) Independent review - internal audit, external assessment. For TISAX: the assessment itself is a compliance verification. Maintain evidence of compliance monitoring between assessments.

clauses.title: Cloud and Hosted Service Controls

clauses.title: Organization of Information Security
clauses.guidance: Establishes roles, responsibilities, and organizational structure for information security. Key elements: (1) Information Security Officer (ISO) - appointed with adequate authority and resources; (2) Segregation of duties - separate conflicting responsibilities; (3) Contact with authorities - procedures for reporting incidents to regulators; (4) Contact with special interest groups - engagement with Auto-ISAC, CERT communities; (5) Project management security - integrate security into all projects. For AL3 assessments, expect auditors to interview the ISO and verify their authority.

clauses.title: Human Resource Security
clauses.guidance: Covers security aspects of employee lifecycle. Key controls: (1) Screening - background checks appropriate to role sensitivity; (2) Terms of employment - security responsibilities in contracts; (3) Awareness training - regular security awareness programs; (4) Disciplinary process - consequences for security violations; (5) Termination procedures - access revocation, asset return. For prototype protection (Proto label), stricter screening may be required. Document training records and maintain evidence of awareness program completion.

clauses.title: Asset Management
clauses.guidance: Requires identification and protection of information assets. Key requirements: (1) Asset inventory - maintain register of all information assets; (2) Ownership - assign owners responsible for asset protection; (3) Acceptable use - define rules for using assets; (4) Classification - label assets by sensitivity (public, internal, confidential, strictly confidential); (5) Media handling - procedures for removable media, disposal. For automotive: include CAD files, test data, calibration parameters, and customer technical information in scope.

clauses.title: Access Control
clauses.guidance: Establishes controls for accessing information systems. Key areas: (1) Access control policy - business requirements for access; (2) User access management - registration, provisioning, review, removal; (3) User responsibilities - password policy, clear desk; (4) System access controls - secure logon, session timeout, privilege restriction; (5) Application access - need-to-know enforcement. For AL3: expect technical verification of access controls, privileged access management, and multi-factor authentication for sensitive systems.

clauses.title: Cryptography
clauses.guidance: Governs use of cryptographic controls. Requirements: (1) Cryptographic policy - when and how to use encryption; (2) Key management - generation, distribution, storage, rotation, destruction; (3) Algorithm selection - use industry-standard algorithms (AES-256, RSA-2048+, SHA-256+); (4) Certificate management - PKI procedures if applicable. For automotive embedded systems: align with AUTOSAR Crypto Stack requirements. Document crypto inventory showing where encryption is used and with what algorithms.

clauses.title: Physical and Environmental Security
clauses.guidance: Protects physical facilities and equipment. Key controls: (1) Secure areas - perimeter security, entry controls, securing offices; (2) Equipment security - siting, protection, maintenance, disposal; (3) Clear desk/screen - policy enforcement; (4) Cabling security - protect power and network cables. For prototype protection: dedicated secure rooms, visitor escort requirements, photography prohibitions, prototype tracking. AL3 assessments include physical site inspection.

clauses.title: Operations Security
clauses.guidance: Covers secure operation of information processing facilities. Key areas: (1) Documented procedures - operating procedures for IT systems; (2) Change management - controlled changes to systems; (3) Capacity management - monitoring and projections; (4) Separation of environments - dev/test/prod separation; (5) Malware protection - anti-virus, endpoint detection; (6) Backup - regular backups with testing; (7) Logging and monitoring - security event logging, log protection; (8) Vulnerability management - scanning, patching. For automotive: include vehicle test systems, HIL/SIL environments in scope.

clauses.title: Communications Security
clauses.guidance: Protects information in networks. Requirements: (1) Network security management - network controls, segregation; (2) Information transfer - policies for email, file transfer, messaging; (3) Confidentiality agreements - NDAs with external parties; (4) Network services security - SLAs with network providers. For supplier data exchange: secure file transfer (SFTP, encrypted email), no customer data on public cloud without approval. VPN requirements for remote access to customer systems.

clauses.title: Prototype Protection
clauses.guidance: Additional requirements for protecting physical prototypes and test vehicles. Key controls: (1) Secure storage - locked facilities, access logging; (2) Transportation - covered transport, GPS tracking for vehicles; (3) Camouflage - disguising prototype features; (4) Photography prohibition - no photos in prototype areas; (5) Visitor management - strict escort requirements; (6) Destruction - secure disposal of prototype components. Required when handling OEM prototype parts or test vehicles. Assessment includes physical inspection of prototype handling areas.

clauses.title: Service authentication profiles
clauses.guidance:
Service authentication profiles in AUTOSAR define standardized patterns for authenticating and authorizing service-oriented communication between ECUs across trust domains. As vehicles transition from signal-based CAN communication to service-oriented Ethernet architectures, authentication must shift from per-message (SecOC) to per-service and per-session models.

Profile Taxonomy. Authentication profiles are categorized by strength and mechanism. Profile None provides no authentication — suitable only for non-security-relevant services within a single trust domain behind firewall protection. Profile PreSharedKey uses symmetric keys shared between service provider and consumer for lightweight mutual authentication — appropriate for intra-domain services with moderate security requirements. Profile Certificate uses X.509 certificate-based mutual TLS authentication — required for cross-domain services and any service accessible from external interfaces. Profile Token uses short-lived authentication tokens issued by a central authentication service — applicable in AUTOSAR Adaptive platforms with centralized identity management.

Profile Assignment Methodology. Each service in the vehicle service catalog should be assigned an authentication profile based on the ISO 21434 TARA results. Services that affect vehicle dynamics (brake, steering, propulsion) require Certificate or Token profiles regardless of network topology. Services carrying personal data (location, driver behavior) require encrypted channels with mutual authentication. Infotainment services within an isolated domain may use PreSharedKey or None with compensating network controls. The profile assignment should be documented as part of the cybersecurity specifications and reviewed during cybersecurity assessment.

Cross-Domain Authentication. When a service call crosses domain boundaries (e.g., an infotainment application requesting vehicle speed data from the powertrain domain), the gateway must enforce the authentication profile defined for that cross-domain service. The caller presents credentials appropriate to the profile, the gateway verifies them (using CSM for cryptographic operations and KeyM for certificate validation), and only then forwards the request to the target domain. Failed authentication attempts should be logged to IdsM as potential unauthorized access attempts.

Platform Integration. In AUTOSAR Adaptive, service authentication integrates with the Identity and Access Management (IAM) framework. The ara::iam service manages authentication state and issues authorization decisions for each service invocation. In AUTOSAR Classic, service authentication is implemented through custom BSW modules or SecOC-based per-PDU authentication on the service transport layer. The choice depends on platform capabilities and performance constraints. R155 evidence should include the service authentication profile mapping and verification test results demonstrating that unauthenticated access to protected services is rejected.

mappings.notes: Service authentication controls should align with external API authorization principles.

clauses.title: Secure diagnostics proxy patterns
clauses.guidance:
Secure diagnostics proxy patterns in AUTOSAR define security controls for routed diagnostic access in architectures where diagnostic requests traverse proxy ECUs before reaching target ECUs. This pattern is common in zonal E/E architectures where a zone controller acts as a diagnostic proxy for ECUs in its zone, and in connected vehicle architectures where remote diagnostics passes through telematics and gateway modules.

Proxy Security Functions. The diagnostics proxy performs several security-critical functions. Authentication enforcement: the proxy verifies the diagnostic client's credentials before forwarding requests to target ECUs, preventing unauthenticated access to ECUs that may lack their own authentication capability. Authorization filtering: the proxy applies policy rules that restrict which diagnostic services and target ECUs a given client can access — remote diagnostic clients may be limited to read-only operations, while workshop clients with physical presence have broader access. Request sanitization: the proxy validates diagnostic request parameters before forwarding, preventing malformed requests that could exploit parsing vulnerabilities in target ECU diagnostic stacks.

Routing Security in Zonal Architectures. In zonal architectures, each zone controller proxies diagnostic access to sensors, actuators, and low-capability ECUs within its zone. The zone controller terminates the external diagnostic session (DoIP/TLS from the diagnostic tester or remote server), authenticates the client, and establishes separate internal diagnostic sessions (CAN UDS) to target ECUs within its zone. This two-stage model means the zone controller holds both the external TLS context and internal CAN authentication state, making it a high-value target. Compromise of a zone controller's diagnostic proxy function could provide an attacker with diagnostic access to all ECUs in that zone.

Remote Diagnostics via SOVD. ISO 17978 (SOVD) introduces service-oriented diagnostic access where diagnostic operations are exposed as REST-like services. When SOVD access passes through a diagnostic proxy, the proxy must enforce SOVD-specific security: OAuth-based token validation for API access, per-operation authorization checks against the SOVD capability model, and rate limiting to prevent resource exhaustion on target ECUs. The proxy must preserve the security context — including caller identity and authorization scope — through the routing chain so that downstream components can make accurate access control decisions.

Audit and Monitoring. All diagnostic proxy operations should be logged with sufficient detail for forensic analysis: timestamp, client identity, target ECU, service requested, parameters, and result. Diagnostic proxy logs are a primary forensic data source when investigating potential unauthorized access. Failed authentication attempts, authorization denials, and unusual request patterns should trigger IdsM security events. R155 threat 7.2.2.2(c) covers unauthorized diagnostic access, and the proxy security architecture with its logging and monitoring capabilities is a key evidence item for type approval.

mappings.notes: Remote diagnostics proxy behavior should remain consistent with SOVD service invocation constraints.

clauses.title: Secure OTA client integration
clauses.guidance:
Secure OTA (Over-The-Air) client integration in AUTOSAR defines how the OTA update client interacts with AUTOSAR security modules to ensure that software updates are delivered and installed with appropriate security controls. The OTA client is the vehicle-side component that receives, validates, and installs software updates — it must integrate tightly with the Crypto Stack, KeyM, Secure Boot, and IdsM.

Update Package Validation. Before any update package is applied, the OTA client must verify its authenticity and integrity. The package is signed by the OEM's code signing infrastructure using asymmetric cryptography (typically ECDSA-P256 or RSA-3072). The OTA client uses CSM to perform signature verification, with the code signing public key stored in a KeyM-managed HSM key slot. The verification chain must extend to the trusted root certificate — the same chain used for secure boot. Update packages should include version metadata that the OTA client validates against the rollback protection counter before proceeding with installation. ISO 24089 Clause 8 defines the distribution and deployment requirements that the OTA client must satisfy.

Secure Download and Storage. Update packages are downloaded over an authenticated and encrypted channel (TLS 1.2+ with mutual authentication). The TLS connection terminates at the telematics control unit or central compute platform, which then distributes package segments to target ECUs via the vehicle's internal network. During download, the OTA client must verify package integrity incrementally (streaming hash verification) rather than waiting for the complete download, to detect corruption or tampering early. Temporary storage of update packages must be in protected memory regions — an attacker with access to the storage medium should not be able to substitute a different update package.

Installation and Activation. The installation process writes the verified update to the target ECU's flash memory using UDS programming services (0x34 RequestDownload, 0x36 TransferData, 0x37 RequestTransferExit). The OTA client must ensure that the programming session is properly authenticated and that the target ECU verifies the received image independently (the target ECU should not trust the OTA client's prior verification alone). After installation, the updated software is activated on next boot, where secure boot verifies the new image before execution. Activation confirmation is sent back through the OTA channel to the backend server.

Failure Handling and Recovery. OTA update failures must not leave the ECU in an inoperable state. A/B partition schemes allow fallback to the previous working version if the new version fails boot verification or self-test. The OTA client must report update status (success, failure with reason, rollback) to both the backend server and IdsM. Update failures due to signature verification errors or unexpected package contents should be flagged as potential security incidents. R155 Annex 5 threat 7.2.3 addresses OTA update security, and the OTA client's integration with AUTOSAR security modules is a primary evidence item for this threat.

mappings.notes: AUTOSAR integration should support governed and secure software distribution workflows.

clauses.title: Domain controller isolation
clauses.guidance:
Domain controller isolation in AUTOSAR defines trust boundaries, memory protection, and communication controls that prevent faults or compromises in one functional domain from affecting other domains. As domain controllers consolidate multiple functions onto single high-performance processors, isolation becomes critical for both safety (ISO 26262 freedom from interference) and security (ISO 21434 defense in depth).

Hardware Isolation Mechanisms. Modern automotive processors provide hardware-enforced isolation through Memory Protection Units (MPU) for Cortex-R cores in AUTOSAR Classic and Memory Management Units (MMU) with virtualization extensions for Cortex-A cores in AUTOSAR Adaptive. Hardware virtualization (Type 1 hypervisor) creates isolated virtual machines, each running a separate AUTOSAR instance with its own memory space, peripheral access, and network interfaces. The hypervisor enforces that a compromised guest VM cannot access memory, peripherals, or network interfaces assigned to other VMs. ARM TrustZone provides an additional isolation boundary, creating a secure world for cryptographic operations and key storage that is inaccessible from the normal world even if the OS is compromised.

Software Isolation in AUTOSAR. AUTOSAR Classic provides OS Application isolation through memory protection boundaries — each OS Application runs in a protected memory region and can only access configured resources. Trusted functions provide controlled privilege escalation for specific operations. AUTOSAR Adaptive uses POSIX process isolation with ara::exec managing process lifecycle and resource allocation. Process-level isolation combined with mandatory access control (based on the platform's IAM configuration) restricts inter-process communication to explicitly authorized channels.

Network Isolation. Domain controllers connected to multiple network segments must enforce network-level isolation between domains. Each virtual domain should have its own virtual Ethernet interface (mapped to VLANs) with firewall rules preventing cross-domain traffic except through explicitly authorized paths. CAN controller access should be partitioned — a function in the body domain should not be able to send CAN messages on the powertrain CAN bus. Network isolation configuration must align with the domain controller's hardware capabilities (number of CAN controllers, Ethernet switch port isolation).

Trust Boundary Enforcement. Each domain boundary represents a trust boundary that must be enforced consistently across hardware, software, and network layers. Cross-domain service calls must pass through authenticated interfaces (service authentication profiles). Shared resources (flash memory, HSM, network bandwidth) must be partitioned with enforceable quotas to prevent resource exhaustion attacks. Diagnostic access to individual domains should be independently authenticated. The trust boundary architecture should be documented as part of the ISO 21434 cybersecurity specifications and mapped to the TARA threat treatment plan, demonstrating how domain isolation mitigates identified cross-domain attack scenarios (R155 7.3.4).

mappings.notes: Controller isolation contributes directly to architecture-level cybersecurity design.

mappings.notes: Session lifecycle security must preserve conductive charging safety assumptions.

clauses.title: Revocation status handling
clauses.guidance: Defines revocation status checking and fallback behavior for certificate-dependent charging workflows.

mappings.notes: Certificate revocation governance principles align with token and identity lifecycle controls.

clauses.title: Cyber incident telemetry
clauses.guidance: Defines telemetry expectations for detecting and reporting charging communication security incidents.

mappings.notes: Incident telemetry should be designed with EVSE interface assumptions in scope.

clauses.title: Timing and flow control
clauses.guidance: Defines coordinated timing and flow-control behavior for robust segmented diagnostics transport.

mappings.notes: Transport timing performance depends on resilient underlying network timing behavior.

clauses.title: Error frame abuse mitigation
clauses.guidance: Defines mitigation strategies for abuse patterns involving transport-layer error behavior.

mappings.notes: IDS rule sets should account for transport-layer abuse signatures.

clauses.title: Secure diagnostic tunneling
clauses.guidance: Defines secure tunneling patterns for forwarding diagnostics payloads across gateways.

mappings.notes: Tunneling behavior should align with diagnostics-over-IP message encapsulation rules.

clauses.title: Secure slave provisioning
clauses.guidance: Defines secure provisioning and configuration controls for LIN slave nodes.

mappings.notes: Provisioning controls should align with cybersecurity implementation and key management.

clauses.title: Schedule integrity protection
clauses.guidance: Defines integrity controls for LIN schedule tables and update propagation.

mappings.notes: Schedule update integrity should follow artifact integrity and packaging governance.

clauses.title: LIN gateway security
clauses.guidance: Defines gateway security controls for LIN-to-CAN/Ethernet bridging points.

mappings.notes: Gateway security posture must be coherent across transport technologies.

clauses.title: Cybersecurity for LSADS
clauses.guidance: Defines cybersecurity assumptions and control expectations for low-speed automated driving systems.

mappings.notes: LSADS architectures should explicitly include cybersecurity design controls.

clauses.title: Low-speed emergency minimum risk condition
clauses.guidance: Defines emergency MRC expectations for low-speed operational domains.

mappings.notes: Emergency behavior triggers should remain aligned with SOTIF trigger assumptions.

clauses.title: Remote assistance security
clauses.guidance: Defines security controls for remote assistance channels used in low-speed ADS operations.

mappings.notes: Remote assistance access control should be governed as an external service authorization path.

clauses.title: Security event reporting
clauses.guidance: Defines reporting controls for security-relevant diagnostics and emissions events.

mappings.notes: Event reporting should integrate with continual cybersecurity monitoring activities.

clauses.title: Cross-protocol gateway governance
clauses.guidance: Defines governance controls where WWH-OBD data traverses protocol and platform boundaries.

mappings.notes: Gateway governance is critical for preserving harmonized diagnostics behavior.

clauses.title: Certificate-scoped diagnostics access
clauses.guidance: Defines certificate-scoped access control requirements for regulated diagnostics operations.

mappings.notes: Certificate-based diagnostics access should follow system cybersecurity design controls.

clauses.title: Cyber scenario injection profiles
clauses.guidance: Defines scenario injection patterns that model cybersecurity-influenced behavior deviations.

mappings.notes: Cybersecurity threat scenarios should be reflected in ADS scenario generation practices.

clauses.title: Scenario data integrity controls
clauses.guidance: Defines integrity and provenance controls for scenario datasets used in validation campaigns.

mappings.notes: Scenario artifact integrity requirements mirror secure distribution controls.

clauses.title: Operational feedback loop
clauses.guidance: Defines feedback loop integration from field operation into scenario catalog maintenance.

mappings.notes: Post-release monitoring should continuously inform scenario portfolio updates.

clauses.title: Evaluation bias detection
clauses.guidance: Defines methods to detect and quantify dataset or process bias in scenario-based safety evaluation.

mappings.notes: Bias detection is needed for defensible validation metric interpretation.

clauses.title: Safety KPI governance
clauses.guidance: Defines governance controls for selecting, baselining, and approving scenario-based safety KPIs.

mappings.notes: KPI governance should support formal safety case evidence integration.

clauses.title: Cyber-safety correlation metrics
clauses.guidance: Defines metrics that capture interactions between cybersecurity conditions and safety outcomes.

mappings.notes: Correlation metrics help integrate cybersecurity evidence into safety evaluation frameworks.

clauses.title: Identifier change governance
clauses.guidance: Defines governance controls for identifier additions, deprecations, and compatibility transitions.

mappings.notes: Identifier lifecycle changes should align with diagnostics dataset governance.

clauses.title: Security-authenticated modes
clauses.guidance: Defines authentication requirements for access to sensitive diagnostic mode data.

mappings.notes: Authenticated diagnostics mode access should be consistent with UDS security access controls.

clauses.title: Emissions data traceability
clauses.guidance: Defines traceability controls linking emitted diagnostics data to regulatory evidence records.

mappings.notes: Traceability controls support harmonized regulatory reporting workflows.

clauses.title: Compliance profile exchange
clauses.guidance: Defines profile exchange structures used to declare diagnostics service capability and compliance constraints.

mappings.notes: Service-oriented diagnostics profile exchange should align with structured diagnostics data packaging.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 9: Incident and response operations
clauses.guidance: Defines incident detection, classification, and coordinated response expectations for connected vehicle operations.

mappings.notes: Incident response operations align with continual cybersecurity activities and post-production monitoring.

clauses.title: Clause 15: Continual conformance assessment
clauses.guidance: Defines periodic conformance assessment activities to maintain update process effectiveness and evidence quality.

mappings.notes: Continual conformance assessment supports SUMS effectiveness expectations.

clauses.title: Security event attribution
clauses.guidance: Defines attribution metadata for emissions diagnostics events with potential cybersecurity relevance.

mappings.notes: Attribution metadata strengthens harmonized reporting of diagnostics security events.
